characters.name: Albrecht of Brandenburg as St. Jerome

event: Cardinal Albrecht of Brandenburg depicted as St. Jerome in his study
symbols: crucifix in the background — the central object of Jerome's devotion and the source of his scholarly labor
open book — the Scriptures that Jerome translated into Latin (the Vulgate)
lion resting at his feet — Jerome's legendary companion, symbol of courage tamed by sanctity
deer — the soul thirsting for God as the deer pants for streams of water (Psalm 42)
cardinal's red robes and hat — Jerome's traditional (anachronistic) depiction as a cardinal of the Church
wilderness setting — Jerome's ascetic withdrawal to the desert for prayer and study
description: A figure in brilliant red cardinal's robes sits at a rough wooden desk in a lush outdoor setting, writing in an open book. A red cardinal's hat hangs on a tree to the left. Behind the figure, a crucifix stands on a hillside near buildings. At the scholar's feet, a lion lies peacefully, and a deer stands nearby. A rabbit sits in the lower left. The surrounding landscape includes dense green trees and a blue sky. The figure wears a red biretta and has a focused, scholarly expression.
theological_description: This painting depicts Cardinal Albrecht of Brandenburg in the guise of St. Jerome, the great fourth-century Doctor of the Church who translated the Bible into Latin, producing the Vulgate. Jerome's association with the wilderness reflects his years of ascetic life in the Syrian desert and his retreat to Bethlehem, where he undertook his monumental translation work. The crucifix prominently displayed recalls Jerome's famous letter in which he described beating his breast with a stone before the cross, crying out for mercy. The lion references the legend that Jerome removed a thorn from a lion's paw, after which the beast became his faithful companion — an allegory of how divine wisdom tames the fiercest passions. The deer alludes to Psalm 42:1, 'As a deer pants for flowing streams, so pants my soul for you, O God,' capturing Jerome's insatiable thirst for divine truth through Scripture. Jerome's legacy is foundational: his insistence that Christians must return to the original Hebrew and Greek texts established the principle of biblical scholarship that would fuel the Reformation a millennium later.

scripture_refs.reason: The deer as symbol of the soul thirsting for God, a key element in Jeromian iconography
The exhortation to rightly handle the word of truth — Jerome as the exemplary biblical scholar
All Scripture is God-breathed — the conviction that drove Jerome's lifelong labor of translation
Your word is a lamp to my feet — the open book representing Scripture as divine illumination

_meta.title: Albrecht of Brandenburg as St. Jerome in his study
